summary:
The Children's Crusade (1212 AD) was a remarkable and deeply debated episode in [[event:92|Crusade]] history, in which large groups of young people — primarily from France and Germany, traditionally described as children but likely including many young adults and impoverished peasants — set out for the Holy Land in spontaneous popular movements led by charismatic young preachers, reportedly believing that God would part the Mediterranean Sea or that their innocence would succeed where the arms of adult knights had failed. The two main movements — led by Stephen of Cloyes in France and Nicholas of Cologne in Germany — both ended in failure and tragedy.

The German contingent led by Nicholas crossed the Alps to Italian ports but found no ships willing to take them to the Holy Land; many died on the mountain crossing, and the survivors dispersed. The French contingent reached Marseille and according to later sources were offered free passage by merchants who then sold them into slavery in North Africa. The historical reality is difficult to disentangle from later legend: contemporary sources are sparse and often contradictory, and the 'Children's Crusade' as typically described may be a later conflation of separate events with some legendary accretion. What seems clear is that the popular religious enthusiasm of 1212 produced spontaneous mass movements of ordinary people toward Jerusalem, reflecting both the genuine popular piety of the period and the degree to which Crusading ideology had penetrated common culture. The episode inspired the modern legend of the Pied Piper of Hamelin and has been frequently interpreted as a symbol of misled innocent idealism.
